title: Async Fire-and-Forget Consumer
description: At-most-once message delivery with no retries, no deduplication, and no delivery confirmation. Optimized for high-throughput, low-latency scenarios where occasional message loss is acceptable (telemetry, metrics, real-time price feeds, IoT sensor data).

cost.costNotes: Lowest-cost messaging pattern: no deduplication store, no retry state, no DLQ required.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min ($10): Low-volume telemetry or metrics pipeline using cloud-managed queue (SNS/SQS, Pub/Sub free tier) with serverless consumers (Lambda free tier). Max ($1,000): High-throughput IoT or real-time feed processing with dedicated consumer instances and high-volume cloud queue usage. No deduplication storage cost since at-most-once accepts duplicates/loss without tracking.
provenance.adoptionCost: Very low complexity: (1) Set up queue/topic with no durability/retention requirements (2-4 hours), (2) Implement simple consumer with no retry or dedup logic (4-8 hours), (3) Basic monitoring for throughput and drop rate (2-4 hours). Total 8-16 hours at $50/hour = $400-800. Simplest of the three delivery guarantee patterns.
provenance.licenseCost: Open-source message brokers (NATS, UDP-based, Redis Pub/Sub) or cloud-managed queues with standard pricing. No deduplication storage required.

provides.reasoning: Core guarantee of this pattern: messages are sent without confirmation or retry, providing at-most-once delivery semantics.
Absence of deduplication lookups, retry state, and acknowledgement overhead allows much higher throughput than at-least-once or exactly-once patterns.

requires.reasoning: Requires a message broker or queue, though lightweight or lossy transports (UDP, Redis Pub/Sub, SNS without SQS) are acceptable.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern is exclusively for at-most-once delivery; using it with at-least-once or exactly-once would silently drop the reliability guarantees those modes require.

requires_constraints.reason: Fire-and-forget pattern is cloud-agnostic; works with any queue or pub/sub infrastructure.
Pattern can be implemented in any language; no library-specific requirements.
Fire-and-forget is most commonly used in telemetry pipelines, IoT data ingestion, and background workers where loss tolerance exists.
At-most-once delivery requires async messaging infrastructure. Setting messaging_delivery_guarantee without enabling async_messaging is a logical contradiction.
Pattern is independent of caching; no dedup cache required.
Pattern supports both multi-tenant and single-tenant deployments.
Fire-and-forget is compatible with both real-time and batch consumption.
Pattern does not require distributed transactions; compatible with transactional systems that emit fire-and-forget side-channel events.
Fire-and-forget is well-suited to real-time streaming workloads (metrics, telemetry) that tolerate loss.
Pattern is agnostic to vector search usage.
Pattern is agnostic to document store usage.
No key-value store required for deduplication; pattern is compatible regardless.
Pattern is agnostic to graph database usage.
Fire-and-forget is a natural fit for feeding time-series databases where occasional gaps are tolerable.
Pattern is compatible with OLTP workloads that emit low-priority async events alongside transactional operations.
Pattern is agnostic to OLAP workload usage.

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern is compatible with any authentication mechanism supported by the underlying queue infrastructure.
Audit logging is optional; fire-and-forget is commonly used for non-sensitive telemetry where logging every message would be prohibitive.
Pattern is compatible with all tenant isolation models; topic/queue-level isolation satisfies multi-tenancy requirements.
Pattern is compatible with both PII and non-PII data; PII workloads require appropriate queue encryption and access controls.
Compatible with GDPR when queue retention and data-subject rights are handled at the infrastructure level.
Compatible with HIPAA when queue encryption and access controls are in place, though message loss may be problematic for audit trails.
Compatible with CCPA for non-sensitive async side-channel events.
SOX workloads should use at-least-once or exactly-once for financial event streams; at-most-once is compatible for non-financial side-channel events only.
Pattern does not provide read-your-writes consistency for message data; compatible with systems that do not require it for async events.

requires_nfr.reason: At-most-once delivery accepts message loss, so the system availability ceiling is lower than retry-based patterns.
Fire-and-forget has minimal latency floor: no dedup lookup, no acknowledgement round-trip. Async enqueue is typically sub-millisecond but network/broker overhead adds ~1ms p95.
Occasional broker backpressure or network jitter adds ~5ms at p99 even without retry overhead.
Consumer restart recovers quickly since no state (dedup store, retry queues) needs to be rebuilt.
At-most-once explicitly accepts in-flight message loss on failure. RPO for message data is unbounded; 60 minutes is a practical minimum acknowledgement of this tradeoff.
No deduplication or retry overhead allows throughput ceiling of ~1M messages/second (3.6B/hour) per consumer cluster.
Fire-and-forget explicitly accepts in-flight message loss on failure. A spec requiring strict durability is incompatible with at-most-once delivery; use at-least-once or exactly-once instead.

acknowledgement_mode.description: Whether to wait for broker acknowledgement. none: pure fire-and-forget, fastest but highest loss risk. broker-ack-only: waits for broker write confirmation but not consumer processing confirmation; reduces broker-side loss without adding consumer retry logic.

retry_attempts.description: Must be 0 for at-most-once semantics. Included for clarity; any value above 0 changes the guarantee to at-least-once.

queue_type.description: Transport type. standard: cloud queue or broker with at-most-once config (e.g., SNS, Redis Pub/Sub). lossy-udp: UDP-based transport for ultra-low latency telemetry (StatsD, syslog). in-memory: in-process channel for same-process async handoff (highest performance, lost on crash).

producer_timeout_ms.description: How long the producer waits before abandoning a send. 0 = non-blocking (true fire-and-forget). Higher values reduce loss under momentary backpressure at cost of producer latency.

batch_size.description: Number of messages to batch before sending. Higher values improve throughput but increase loss window on producer crash.

warn_nfr.reason: Fire-and-forget adds async infrastructure overhead. At <1 msg/s (3600/hour), simpler synchronous patterns may be more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: async-fire-and-forget: peak_jobs_per_hour is {actual} (<3600/hour = <1 msg/s). Async messaging overhead may outweigh benefit for low-volume workloads.

warn_nfr.reason: At-most-once delivery accepts message loss; achieving four-nines availability for message delivery is contradictory to the pattern's loss-tolerance model.
warn_nfr.message: async-fire-and-forget: spec requires availability target of {actual} (≥99.99%), but at-most-once delivery explicitly accepts message loss. Consider at-least-once or exactly-once patterns for high-availability workloads.
